# Transcrição: 02 Introduction

- **Fonte:** 02 Introduction
- **Duração:** 05:23
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Let's get into the specifics. The intro. This begins the story of when you hit rock bottom. And it happens before discovering what you're going to share with your reader, whatever your solution may be. That's how this starts off. And here's how you create it. You're going to create this using what I call the specifics strategy. Here I want you to start taking a lot of notes. I'm going to go slow to make sure that you get it all. You're going to ask yourself, when was it? When was your personal event? I want to know the date down to the day and time, if possible. Literally. Specifics create fantastic copy. And anyone can be specific. This is one of a copywriter's most secret cherished tricks of the trade is copywriters love, they hold this back. But if you look at any great story, it's always really specific. If you read a great novel, it covers every little detail. That's what makes it great. Well, you can do this. And here's how you start. When was it? The date down to the time. What was the weather like? Now, people might think, well, who cares what the weather was like? Well, I'm going to show you a couple of examples of why you should care. Let's say it was raining. I was driving and I was scared that I might crash so you can build a little bit of tension. It was a sunny, beautiful day. And if you're selling a weight loss product, you know, everybody else was probably at the beach, but I hated my body so much, I couldn't enjoy the beautiful sunny day. I remember that. So why did you remember it was a beautiful sunny day? Because you remembered you couldn't go to the beach. It was cloudy and gloomy. And maybe that weather reflected my depression over whatever it is, the bad part, the focal point, this depression that you're about to enter into. That's why the weather is important. Plus, when you mention the weather, the story becomes palpable. I mean, it becomes something people can touch and feel. If you say, I remember this Tuesday, you know, it was Tuesday sometime in November. And I remember that it was really, really cloudy that day. So you're telling a story that is really pulling people into the narrative. What were you wearing? This is some great examples. Two jackets. I was wearing two jackets because I was trying to hide my gut. I had one tight jacket underneath and then the big jacket on top. I actually did that once, by the way. I was wearing clothes from Kmart. And not because I was a smart shopper, but because I was a broke shopper. Now, this lets people, you're going to hear me talk about this throughout this whole entire course. You want to let the reader off the hook. You want to let the reader off the hook just long enough to make them realize that they need to be on the hook. And you're going to offer them a hook along with the bait, along with the price. Okay, that is your grand offer. But they have to be let off the hook a little bit because they feel pretty crappy right now. Okay, so for example, if someone has to shop at Kmart and they don't have the money to shop somewhere else, you compliment them for being a smart shopper. But in your case, it wasn't because I was smart, because it is kind of smart to shop at Kmart some of the time, but it was because I was broke. You see how that paints a vivid picture?

## [03:02 - 05:23]

Next, who were you with? If it's a weight loss product, my skinny friend, Lisa, if she was skinny, that's great. So in that actually is from a real story, not for me, but from something I wrote. Alone as usual. If you have a dating product, a relationship building product, that's powerful. Alone as usual. Or if you have a financial product, you could say I was with my financial advisor, James, just whoever you were with, it doesn't even matter if it's an important to the story. In my personal story, it has no bearing on the story at all. But I use Steve because I was with Steve. And when I tell my audience that I was with my friend Steve, it makes my story that much more believable. First of all, because that is the truth. And second of all, it makes it that much more real. The goal here is to pull your reader into a state of dismissing. Remember, we're trying to dismiss that decision mode and into literally absorbing them into a story. And this is how you do it. The more real, the more factual, the more little details and strategic details that you put in, the better. Next is where were you? I was at the downtown office and we have a joke in here. I was in Greenbow, Alabama. Forest Gump joke on the side of the road on I-10. Get very specific. Where was I? What city? What road? If it matters, you can get as specific as you want here. Where were you? And what were you feeling up until this moment? The moment right before the big focal point happens. I was excited because it was, I just had a birthday as an example. So you were feeling excited. Or what if you were scared? I was scared of being caught daydreaming again by my boss. Okay, so this is what you were feeling before something really tragic happened. Okay? In my case, I was feeling anxious and I was feeling hungry. That's my personal story. So it can be anything you were feeling at that moment. Now, I'm going to use the word feeling a lot because why? Because you bond through feeling. You do not bond through logic. We're going to write logically because you want to attract them logical buyer. I'll show you how in another session. But you're going to write primarily emotionally, especially during the nightmare story and the dream story, because that's what people relate to the strongest.
